title: What remains: the collected poems of Hannah Arendt [Book Review]
author: Tang

opening_move.description: The review opens by asking who Arendt's inner interlocutors were and in what language they spoke — using the multilingual, itinerant quality of her poetry to locate a 'poetic canvas' of her political thinking.
opening_move.stakes_claimed: Arendt's poems reveal a dimension of her political thought that cannot be recovered from her philosophical prose alone — the poetic thinking that underlies her pluralism

theoretical_positioning.description: The review is broadly appreciative and interpretive, presenting the collection as revealing an Arendt at the intersection of poetic tradition and political thought, without substantial critique.

epistemic_mode.interpretive_methods: literary and philosophical hermeneutics; close reading of Arendt's poetry and prose
epistemic_mode.notes: Book review genre; predominantly literary-interpretive. The normative argument (if any) is implicit: recovering the full Arendt enriches our understanding of political pluralism.

case_type.case_name: Arendt, What Remains: Collected Poems (1923-1961), trans. Hill and Grill

confidence_notes: Book review of a poetry edition; atypically literary for this journal. Classified as purely_interpretive_hermeneutic given near-absence of normative argument. Low normative stake reflects literary rather than political-theoretical focus.
